frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
Suresh: "Bhai, dekho toh. Aajkal ki chhoriyan na, shaadi toh kar leti hain par maang mein sindoor lagana bhi bhool jaati hain. Arre, wohi toh suhagan ki pehchan hai, ghar ki shobha." 
Relative: "Haan bhai, sahi keh raha hai. Lagta hai inko sab modern banna hai. Par apni sanskriti ka bhi toh maan rakhna chahiye."
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Suresh expresses disapproval of a young married woman not wearing sindoor, framing it as a departure from tradition and a failure to uphold the 'mark of a married woman' and 'glory of the home'. This encodes the core belief that a married woman should visibly signal her marital status and judges those who don't, linking it to cultural disloyalty and lack of respect for tradition.
original_frame: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 45. Married. A Jat man in Haryana. You are sitting with three male relatives at a family function after news broke in your district that a young woman from a nearby village was assaulted while walking home from a tuition class at 7pm. You are talking about what happened and what you think the girl's family should have done and what girls in villages need to understand about going out alone even in the evening.
